criteria: The agent has read the Abstract and Introduction to identify the core proposal: applying a head-specific sigmoid gate after the Scaled Dot-Product Attention (SDPA) to improve performance, training stability, and eliminate attention sinks.
The plan must outline a strategy to compare at least 30+ variants, involving both 15B Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) and 1.7B dense models
The roadmap must explicitly include a task to "disentangle" the gating effect from other factors (like routing in Switch Heads or sparsity in NSA)

The agent has read Section 2.2 to extract the core gating equation $Y^{\prime} = Y \odot \sigma(XW_{\theta})$ and identify that gating at the SDPA output ($G_1$) yields the most significant improvements compared to other positions ($G_2$ to $G_5$
The plan details the implementation of 5 specific gating positions ($G_1$ to $G_5$) and specifies "Multiplicative Sigmoid" as the primary configuration to be tested.
The plan includes a comparative setup between "Elementwise" (per-dimension) and "Headwise" (per-head) gating scores to determine the optimal precision.

The code accurately implements the core mathematical equation for multiplicative gating $Y^{\prime} = Y \odot \sigma(XW_{\theta})$, using the pre-normalized hidden states $X$ to compute the query-dependent gating scores.

The implementation properly handles tensor reshaping and broadcasting within the Group Query Attention (GQA) framework, correctly defining the learnable projection matrix dimensions for both Elementwise ($n \times q \times d_k$) and Headwise ($n \times q$).
The codebase includes modular forward-pass hooks or conditional branches to dynamically inject the gating mechanism at the specified positions: post-SDPA ($G_1$) and post-Value ($G_2$).

The modified transformer forward pass executes successfully during both training and inference without raising Out-of-Memory (OOM) exceptions or tensor shape mismatches, specifically when broadcasting the gating scores across the Group Query Attention (GQA) heads.

The agent has read Section 3.2.1 to observe the ablation results, noting that multiplicative elementwise/headwise gating outperforms additive gating, and that enforcing head-shared gating diminishes performance.

The agent has read Section 3.2.2 to extract the finding that SDPA output gating significantly enhances training stability for dense models, nearly eliminating loss spikes and enabling the use of larger learning rates.
The plan specifies training on high-quality 3.5T - 4T token datasets with a sequence length of 4096 and GQA (Group Query Attention) architecture.

The strategy includes a specific test case to increase the Learning Rate (from $4.5 \times 10^{-3}$ to $8.0 \times 10^{-3}$) to verify if the gate effectively mitigates loss spikes compared to the baseline.
The model initialization script correctly scales down the Feed-Forward Network (FFN) hidden dimension width to strictly maintain the same total parameter count when the gating mechanism is added

The training pipeline includes a configurable learning rate scheduler capable of executing the stability stress test, seamlessly supporting a peak learning rate of $8.0 \times 10^{-3}$ without throwing initialization errors.

The dense model training script runs to completion under the stress-test configuration (maximum learning rate of $8.0 \times 10^{-3}$ and batch size of 4096) without encountering loss spikes, NaNs, or model divergence.

The evaluation results must match the reported perplexity (PPL) trends from Table 1, confirming that the SDPA elementwise gating ($G_1$) achieves a significantly lower PPL compared to both the baseline and other gating positions like $G_4$ or $G_5$.

The logged training loss curves must reproduce the stability improvements shown in Figure 1 (right), clearly demonstrating the elimination of severe loss spikes when trained with higher learning rates (e.g., $8.0 \times 10^{-3}$).

The agent has read Section 4.1 to point out the theoretical bottleneck: consecutive $W_V$ and $W_O$ layers form a low-rank linear mapping, and gating addresses this by increasing expressiveness through non-linearity.
The agent has read Section 4.2 to identify that effective gating scores are highly sparse and specifically query-dependent, which helps effectively filter out context irrelevant to the current query.

The agent has read Section 4.3 to understand that query-dependent sparse gating at the SDPA output effectively eliminates "massive activations" and consequently resolves the "attention sink" phenomenon on initial tokens.

The agent has read Section 4.4 to extract the impact on context length extension, noting that gated models significantly outperform standard baselines at 64k and 128k sequence lengths on the RULER benchmark when using YaRN.

The agent has read Appendix A.1 to observe that a simplified Switch Head setup (Switch v 1top1) performs very well, proving that the gating mechanism itself provides intrinsic value separate from expert routing.
The plan outlines a method to compare the gated attention against "Group Norm/RMSNorm" and "SiLU-only" variants to isolate the effect of non-linearity on low-rank mapping.
he plan mandates the collection of "F-Attn" (First-token attention) and "M-Act" (Max Activation) metrics across all layers to verify the elimination of attention sinks.
The plan details a 3-step extrapolation process: (1) Extend RoPE base to 1M, (2) Continue training for 80B tokens at 32k length, (3) Apply YaRN for 128k length inference.
he plan lists the RULER benchmark as the primary metric for long-context performance, specifically focusing on the 64k and 128k sequence length thresholds.
The codebase implements logic to mathematically compute and return the maximum absolute activation values (M-Act) of the hidden states at each transformer layer during the forward pass.

The attention module is modified to return the raw attention probability matrices, along with a utility script to programmatically calculate the average attention mass allocated to the first token (F-Attn) across all layers and heads
The code correctly implements the Non-Sparse sigmoid function (NS-sigmoid) defined as $0.5 + 0.5 \cdot \sigma(x)$ as an interchangeable activation option to ablate the effect of gating sparsity.
The codebase includes baseline ablation layers at the SDPA output, specifically implementing standard RMSNorm and a SiLU-only pass (without extra parameters) to isolate the non-linearity effects.
The positional embedding script modifies the standard RoPE implementation to support dynamically altering the base frequency up to 1,000,000.
The inference code successfully integrates the YaRN (Yet another RoPE extensioN) algorithm, implementing length scaling and temperature adjustments to support extrapolation up to 128k tokens.

The diagnostic inference script for computing massive activations (M-Act) and first-token attention (F-Attn) executes successfully over the full test dataset, correctly aggregating layer-wise metrics without causing memory leaks.4

The scripts for running ablation variants (Head-Shared gating, NS-sigmoid, and SiLU) execute sequentially and log results correctly without state-contamination or parameter bleed-over between individual runs.

The long-context evaluation script executes flawlessly up to 128k tokens using YaRN on the RULER benchmark without crashing due to context length limits, recursion errors, or KV-cache memory overflow.

The implementation results must explicitly reproduce the elimination of the attention sink phenomenon (Table 4 and Figure 2), demonstrating that the average attention score on the first token (F-Attn) drastically drops from approximately 46.7% to around 4.8%.

The diagnostic logs must confirm the significant reduction of massive activations (M-Act), showing that the SDPA elementwise gate prevents the extreme hidden state values (e.g., dropping from >1000 down to ~94) seen in the baseline's middle layers.

The ablation output evaluation must confirm that applying Head-Shared gating or the Non-Sparse sigmoid (NS-sigmoid) diminishes overall performance and fails to reduce massive activations, perfectly matching the analysis presented in Section 4.2 and Table 4.

The codebase must validate the long-context extrapolation capabilities, confirming that models trained with SDPA gating maintain higher accuracy on the RULER benchmark at the 64k and 128k sequence lengths compared to standard baselines (aligning with Table 5).
